Eightieth session – Agenda item 151 – Financing of the United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus – Budget performance for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus – Report of the Secretary-General
Contents

The present report contains the budget performance and the budget for the United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus (UNFICYP) for the periods from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027, respectively.
During the 2024/25 period, the operations of the Force remained stable within the overall objective of maintaining peace and security in Cyprus and facilitating a return to normal conditions.
During the 2026/27 period, UNFICYP will continue its efforts focused on preventing tensions in the buffer zone and enhancing trust-building and cooperation between communities across the island.

In the 2024/25 period, UNFICYP incurred $55.3 million in expenditure for the budget performance period, representing a resource utilization rate of 97.9 per cent, compared with $56.1 million in expenditure and a resource utilization rate of 99.8 per cent in the 2023/24 period.

The unencumbered balance of $1.2 million reflects the reduced operational costs due to: (a) the reduced requirements under military and police personnel, attributable primarily to the lower costs of rations and travel for rotation of military contingent personnel; and (b) the reduced requirements for operational costs attributable primarily to the deferral of acquisitions and some services, fewer flight hours resulting from the measures implemented to address liquidity challenges, offset in part by (c) the increased requirements for civilian personnel attributable primarily to the higher actual separation costs for retiring international staff and the application of the revised salary scale for national staff.

The budget proposed for the 2026/27 period in the amount of $56.3 million represents a decrease of $1.0 million, or 1.8 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $57.3 million approved for the 2025/26 period.

The reduced requirements for the 2026/27 period are attributable primarily to the lower requirements for uniformed personnel and international staff owing to the application of higher vacancy rates than those approved and the proposed abolishment of civilian personnel posts. The reduced requirements are offset in part by: (a) the higher costs of utilities and maintenance services; and (b) the higher salary rates for civilian personnel.
(Thousands of United States dollars; budget year is from 1 July to 30 June)
The proposed budget provides for the deployment of uniformed personnel and civilian staff as follows:

a	Represents the highest level of authorized/proposed strength.

Legislative history
(Thousands of United States dollars)

The actions to be taken by the General Assembly are set out in section IV of the present report.

text: I.	Mandate, results achieved and planned outcome

children.text: A.	Mandate

1.	The mandate of UNFICYP was established by the Security Council in its resolution 186 (1964). The mandate for the performance period was provided in its resolutions 2674 (2023) and 2723 (2024). The most recent extension of the mandate was authorized by the Council in its resolution 2815 (2026), by which it extended the mandate until 31 January 2027.
2.	The Force is mandated to help the Security Council achieve the overall objective of advancing peace and security in Cyprus and a return to normal conditions.
B.	Budget implementation for 2024/25

3.	During the 2024/2025 performance period, UNFICYP enhanced its efforts in all areas of its mandate, including preventing tensions and maintaining a calm and stable environment on the ground, political engagements and supporting initiatives aimed at trust-building and cooperation.

4.	UNFICYP continued to support confidence-building measures through the facilitation and co-facilitation of technical committees, the functioning of the Joint Contact Room and regular engagement with the sides on issues of mutual concern, including the clearance of minefields and suspected hazardous areas.

5.	UNFICYP also expanded gender-inclusive peacebuilding activities, emphasizing youth- and women-led initiatives, especially outside Nicosia, and engaged proactively with partners, including the diplomatic community, on a range of issues focused on advancing the women and peace and security and youth and peace and security agendas as part of the broader peace process.

6.	Coordination with the United Nations country team, including the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR), the International Organization for Migration (IOM) and the Committee on Missing Persons in Cyprus was instrumental in creating an environment conducive to peace. The Force provided logistical and substantive support to the Office of the Special Adviser to the Secretary-General on Cyprus and collaborated with international actors such as the European Union to reinforce peacebuilding efforts. The Force’s strategy continued to prioritize enhancing situational awareness, reducing tensions and improving operational efficiency.

7.	The Force prioritized surveillance upgrades in the buffer zone, including expanded closed-circuit television coverage at nine sites within the buffer zone and the deployment of bodycams, trap-cams and dash-cams to better document violations. Environmental sustainability remained a key priority, with the installation of one new photovoltaic power plant at observation post 08 and the replacement of outdated electrical appliances.
8.	Infrastructure improvements included the continued refurbishment of the perimeter fence around the United Nations Protected Area and upgrades to helicopter landing sites.

9.	Despite these achievements, several external factors impeded the full implementation of the mandate. The absence of meaningful progress towards the resumption of formal negotiations, limited intercommunal contact and violations of varying severity contributed to persistent tensions and posed challenges to the Force’s mandate delivery in the buffer zone and broader efforts to strengthening intercommunal dialogue and collaboration.

10.	At the operational level, while the United Nations-owned vehicle replacement plan was advanced to some extent, UNFICYP faced constraints due to the continued reliance on ageing vehicles, which could not be reduced without compromising mandate delivery, given the high proportion of national staff requiring mobility for operational duties. Rising costs, particularly for commercial flights and civilian personnel, also impacted budget implementation. These challenges underscored the need for sustained resource provision and flexible planning in order to adapt to evolving operational realities.
C.	Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives for 2026/27

11.	In the 2026/27 period, UNFICYP will continue to prioritize efforts that contribute to the prevention of a recurrence of fighting, the maintenance of law and order and a return to normal conditions.

12.	The Force will continue to take a proactive political engagement approach and direct all its efforts towards creating conditions conducive to a political settlement. In that regard, UNFICYP will continue to perform integrated and coordinated military and civilian liaison in handling tensions and maintaining a calm and stable environment, as well as the integrity of the buffer zone. These efforts will be conducted through military and police patrols, liaison, engagement and coordination with both sides. UNFICYP will also continue to use the media to effectively increase awareness in order to reduce tensions between the sides. Enhanced situational awareness and data-driven analysis and decision-making on the developments in the buffer zone will also remain a priority.

13.	UNFICYP will continue to support confidence-building measures, including through the facilitation and co-facilitation of seven technical committees, including the newly established Technical Committee on Youth, as well as ad hoc measures negotiated with the sides. The Force will continue to encourage the parties to clear the remaining 29 minefields, including 4 in the buffer zone, and nine suspected hazardous areas, to generate confidence in the peace process and achieve a mine-free Cyprus.

14.	The Security Council, in its resolution 2771 (2025), urged the leaders to increase their support to, and ensure a meaningful role for, civil society engagement in peace efforts, in particular strengthening the participation of women’s organizations and young people in the process. UNFICYP will continue its support for civil society, with an emphasis on trust-building between the communities and on the inclusion of women and young people, and will leverage its decentralized structure to increase its reach to and work with local organizations outside Nicosia. The Force will continue to carry out outreach in an integrated manner by assessing applications and issuing permits, as well as facilitating humanitarian assistance and meetings on religious matters.

15.	Pursuant to Security Council resolution 1325 (2000) and all subsequent resolutions on women and peace and security, UNFICYP will continue to support efforts for the full, meaningful and effective participation of women in peace and political processes and to bring together a broader range of women actors on both sides. In the 2026/27 period, the Force will continue to focus on gender-inclusive peacebuilding activities with youth- and women-led peacebuilding efforts across the island, in particular outside Nicosia.

16.	The Comprehensive Planning and Performance Assessment System will continue to be used on a regular basis to plan, assess, adapt and strengthen operations to enhance mandate delivery. UNFICYP will continue to use the data and analysis to develop and view strategies and monitor performance and better illustrate to Member States how the Force is contributing to change over time and where it faces challenges, including through reports of the Secretary-General and Security Council briefings.
Mission support initiatives

17.	The support component will continue to provide the logistical, administrative and security services necessary for UNFICYP to implement its mandate. The major assumptions underlying the operations and plans of the Force during the 2026/27 period are driven in large part by the provision of the resources necessary to effectively and fully implement the mandate of the Force, while taking initiatives to achieve further efficiencies, where feasible.

18.	UNFICYP will continue its commitment to reducing its environmental footprint through the installation of the ninth photovoltaic power plant in sector 1, combined with the gradual replacement of obsolete electrical appliances such as air conditioning units with eco-friendly units.

19.	UNFICYP will continue to strengthen the safety and security of the Force’s military, police and civilian personnel with the phased implementation of five projects for the improvement of helicopter landing sites in the buffer zone and the completion of the United Nations Protected Area fencing project.
20.	UNFICYP will continue its support for the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy within the Force through the implementation of disability access across all areas of Force operations.

21.	UNFICYP will continue the implementation of the vehicle replacement plan and proposes the acquisition of 15 vehicles for the replacement of 13 light passenger vehicles, 1 truck and 1 forklift in the 2026/27 period which have reached the end of their operational useful lives. The requirement for the replacement of vehicles is based on repair history, technical assessment of remaining economic life, the cost and extent of required repairs, age, condition and overall safety.

22.	With regard to communications and information technology, the Force plans: (a) to extend the coverage of the current closed-circuit television network by installing additional camera systems in critical operational locations throughout the buffer zone, to assist in the observation of unauthorized activity; (b) to upgrade and replace closed-circuit television cameras that have exceeded their useful lives; and (c) to replace the door access systems for all offices.

23.	In line with the Secretary-General’s UN80 Initiative, the proposed budget reflects targeted efficiency measures through organizational optimization, functional streamlining and improved alignment of resources with priorities. UNFICYP proposes adjustments to personnel, including reduced deployment of military contingent personnel, military staff officers and United Nations police officers and abolishment of posts, while maintaining a focus on the effective delivery of mandated activities. These adjustments reflect the prioritization of operational requirements and optimized deployment, enabling essential outcomes to be delivered with a leaner footprint.

24.	These efficiency measures are supported by streamlined organizational structures, including the redistribution of responsibilities and simplified workflows, strengthened coordination between uniformed and civilian components and enhanced utilization of Regional Service Centre in Entebbe (RSCE) shared services.

25.	While flexibility to respond to peaks in demand may be more constrained, the Mission will continue to apply risk-based prioritization and adaptive planning and strengthen management oversight to mitigate any potential risks. The impact of these measures will be closely monitored and adjusted as necessary to sustain effective mandate implementation.
Civilian staffing review

26.	The Force and the Department of Operational Support conducted a civilian staffing review in the 2024/25 period pursuant to General Assembly resolution 76/274. The review was focused on aligning structures and functions with operational priorities and identifying opportunities to enhance coordination and streamline roles. It was strongly guided by the maximizing of efficiencies, and its recommendations were therefore aimed at optimizing the use of existing capacities, including through improved integration and communication.

27.	The civilian staffing review also served to assess the existing structure and staffing in alignment with the guidance on mission support structure and the budgetary directives. The recommendations included a more strategically aligned and capability-driven staffing structure to strengthen mission support, enhance integration across the civilian, military and police components and address key operational gaps. The review contained suggestions for dedicated resources for environmental management, the augmentation of air operations with flight follower roles, integrated approaches to data analytics and workforce upskilling in line with the United Nations 2.0 vision, which the Force will address within existing resources. The civilian staffing review contained recommendations that UNFICYP continue to explore the possibility of transferring location-independent, transactional functions to shared services to improve efficiency and optimize resources and of regularizing the currently existing two-pillar mission support structure. All recommendations were developed within existing budget ceilings, indicating that any proposed increase in resources must be offset by reductions.
28.	UNFICYP welcomed the recommendations contained in the civilian staffing review and proposes a number of staffing changes in the present report.
D.	Regional mission cooperation

29.	During the 2024/25 period, UNFICYP continued to engage closely with other United Nations missions and offices in the region, including the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL), the United Nations Disengagement Observer Force (UNDOF), the United Nations Truce Supervision Organization (UNTSO), the Office of the United Nations Special Coordinator for Lebanon and the Office of the United Nations Special Coordinator for the Middle East Peace Process. This cooperation ensured consistency in approaches across missions and promoted collective responses to regional challenges. In particular, UNIFIL, through its Regional Field Technology Section, provided UNFICYP with strategic guidance in coordinating and implementing field technology services in line with United Nations Headquarters guidelines. These efforts helped reduce disparities in service delivery, generate economies of scale and minimize duplication of effort across missions.

30.	UNFICYP also sought assistance from UNIFIL in areas such as HIV/AIDS counselling, conduct and discipline matters and strengthening regional cooperation on prevention activities, including induction briefings and mandatory training sessions on these issues. This collaboration reinforced mission-wide efforts to prevent sexual exploitation and abuse, sexual harassment and fraud, while supporting targeted training aligned with strategies to address prohibited conduct. In addition, the Force benefited from the support of the Kuwait Joint Support Office, in which one General Service staff member is embedded to process payroll for national staff and individual uniformed personnel, ensuring administrative efficiency and continuity.

31.	During the 2026/27 period, UNFICYP will continue the same regional cooperation as in the 2024/25 period, apart from the cooperation with the Kuwait Joint Support Office. After the closure of the Kuwait Joint Support Office, on 30 June 2026, the services thereafter would be provided by the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe.
E.	Human resources
Executive direction and management

children.text: a	Funded under general temporary assistance.
F.	Proposed staffing changes

32.	Following the recommendation contained in the civilian staffing review, it is proposed to formalize the current two-pillar structure comprising the Operations and Resource Management pillar and the Supply Chain Management and Service Delivery pillar. The merger of the Supply Chain and Service Delivery pillars has been operationally implemented and proven to be functional under resource constraints. However, with these two pillars being merged, it is important to ensure that organizational units managing funds in service delivery are separate from the units performing procurement functions, in order to avoid conflict of interest and to enforce internal control. It is therefore proposed to move the Procurement Section to the Operations and Resource Management pillar to align the organizational structure with procurement policy principles, preserving the segregation of duties and mitigating procurement-related risk exposure.

33.	In addition, to ensure leadership continuity, operational resilience and adequate oversight across diverse functional areas, it is proposed to establish a post of Chief of Operations and Resource Management at the P-5 level, which will fill the notable gap resulting from the absence of dedicated Chief of Operations and Resource Management capacity. The establishment of this post will be offset by the abolishment of the post of Chief of Service Delivery (P-4) to ensure neutrality in the proposed post actions.

34.	It is proposed to abolish two international Field Services posts and six national General Service posts. The functions associated with the abolished posts will be absorbed by the remaining posts and positions, allowing the Mission to operate with a leaner structure while preserving essential capacities to sustain operational effectiveness.

35.	It is also proposed to abolish three national General Service posts which provided location independent transactional functions and one national General Service post that was embedded in the Kuwait Joint Support Office which provided services for the processing of payroll for the Force’s national staff and military staff officers. These functions will be transferred to the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe to benefit from greater efficiencies and effectiveness and following the planned closure of the Office on 30 June 2026.
36.	Lastly, it is proposed to retain two general temporary assistance positions approved for the 2025/26 period:

(a)	The position of Programme Management Officer (P-4), established on 1 July 2022 to direct the implementation of the mine action programme of UNFICYP. In view of the continuation of the programme in the 2026/27 period, it is proposed that the position be retained. The incumbent develops the mission’s mine action programme strategy, leads operational planning, programme design and responses to threats, supports advocacy efforts and oversees the delivery and performance of implementing partners;

(b)	The position of Air Operations Officer (Field Service) as head of the unit is also to be retained. The Aviation Unit monitors, reports and tracks the Force’s aviation activities and is responsible for risk management and quality control of air operations.
Proposed staffing changes

Component 1: political and civil affairs

table_content.text: 1.1	Tensions in the buffer zone between Greek Cypriot and Turkish Cypriot communities are contained by creating a conducive environment for the peace process

1.1.1	Number of issues related to the buffer zone that require escalation to political levels with the sides, as appropriate, are reduced (2022/23: 7; 2023/24: 6; 2024/25: 15; 2025/26:10; 2026/27: 10)
1.1.2	Management of civilian activity in the buffer zone through the issuance of permits (2022/23: 2,387; 2023/24: 2,345; 2024/25: 2,000; 2025/26; 2,000; 2026/27: 2,000)
1.2	Tensions resulting from the ongoing division of the island are mitigated in key areas through the opening of avenues for cooperation and trust-building
1.2.1	All issues raised by the minority communities on both sides are resolved (2022/23: 49; 2023/24: 71; 2024/25: 27; 2025/26: yes; 2026/27: yes)
1.2.2	Maintain established mechanisms for alleviating tensions between the sides and addressing island-wide concerns (2022/23: 23; 2023/24: 15; 2024/25: 9; 2025/26: 9; 2026/27: 9)
1.2.3	Confirmed minefields in the buffer zone are cleared (2022/23: 0; 2023/24: 0; 2024/25: 4; 2025/26: 4; 2026/27: 4)
1.3	Opportunities created for increased and enhanced intercommunal contact and civil society engagement in the peace process

1.3.1	Number of social and cultural events and political and civil society meetings under UNFICYP facilitation that contribute to improved trust-building and intercommunal relations and the strengthening of a peace constituency on the island (2022/23: 643; 2023/24: 914; 2024/25: 600; 2025/26 600; 2026/27: 600)

1.3.2	Number of intercommunal events (organized or supported by UNFICYP) that integrate a gender dimension and promote the involvement of women in the peace process (2022/23: 25; 2023/24: 14; 2024/25: not applicable; 2025/26:45; 2026/27: 45)
1.4	Increased situational awareness of factors affecting the implementation of the Force’s mandate through a Joint Mission Analysis Centre

1.4.1	Issuance of analytical products and predictive assessments to support evidence-based senior leadership decision-making and management of the mission-wide information collection plan (2022/23: 12; 2023/24: 61; 2024/25: 12; 2025/26: 12; 2026/27: 12)

children.text: Evaluation activities

37.	The following evaluations completed by the Office of Internal Oversight Services during the 2024/25 period have guided UNFICYP planning for the 2026/27 period: evaluation of the United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus, 27 May 2025, and the contribution of strategic communications to fostering public trust in peacekeeping operations, 9 August 2024.
38.	No external evaluations are planned for the 2026/27 period.

Note: The amounts represent the total obligations that were cancelled at the end of the 12-month period following the end of the budget period in respect of goods supplied and services rendered in the budget period, and at the end of an additional period of four years in respect of obligations to troop- and police-contributing countries.
F.	Other revenue and adjustments, and borrowing
1.	Other revenue and adjustments
(Thousands of United States dollars)

43.	Credits comprising the unencumbered balances, other revenue and the cancellation of prior-period obligations reduce the assessed contributions owed by Member States in the following financial year. The credit for the 2024/25 period for overall peacekeeping operations amounts to $335.5 million, including the support entities and MINUSMA. In the current financial situation, offsetting this large amount of credit against a future assessment for the 2026/27 period will reduce the collection and undermine the ability of peacekeeping operations to fully implement their mandates. Accordingly, the Secretary-General proposes that the General Assembly approve that the credits in respect of the financial period 2024/25 be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the implementation of those mandates and allow Member States to apply their share towards current or future dues once the Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has sufficiently improved. This proposal is explained in more detail in the peacekeeping overview report.
2.	Borrowing
44.	Owing to cash liquidity situations in other active peacekeeping missions, the Force issued loans to missions, with the month-end loan balance as follows:
(Millions of United States dollars)
Abbreviation: UNSOS, United Nations Support Office in Somalia.
G.	Vacancy factors

45.	The cost estimates for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 take into account the vacancy factors outlined in the table below. The table also summarizes, by category, the actual rates in the periods from 2022/23 to 2024/25, as well as the approved and current rates for the 2025/26 period.
(Percentage)
a	Funded under general temporary assistance.

46.	The vacancy factors take into account the high number of abolishments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27. Accordingly, the proposed vacancy rates for civilian personnel categories with abolishments were estimated using the total number of vacant posts and positions as at 31 December 2025, net of the number of posts and positions proposed for abolishment. For categories without proposed abolishments, the standard policy guidance was applied, using either the 12 months average vacancy rate from January to December 2025 or the actual vacancy rate as at 31 December 2025. For military and police personnel, the vacancy factors reflect their planned deployment during the period. This approach ensures that vacancy rates are based on, to the extent possible, the actual data while maintaining consistency across the proposed budgets. A vacancy rate of 50 per cent has been applied in the calculation of costs for the proposed establishment.
H.	Analysis of requirements for air operations

47.	The flight hour utilization rates for the periods from 2022/23 to 2024/25, the approved and proposed number of flight hours for the 2025/26 and 2026/27 periods, respectively, and the number of flight hours calculated on the basis of the new policy guidance are as follows:

48.	UNFICYP will continue to deploy three aircraft to facilitate the provision of surveillance and deterrence in its area of operations. The proposed deployment also takes into consideration medical or casualty evacuations, as well as personnel and cargo transportation capabilities. The number of flight hours is in line with the policy guidance and is based on the mission’s pattern of flight utilization.

49	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) lower cost for rations owing to the lower actual average ceiling for individual daily rate compared with the rates applied in the approved budget; and (b) lower actual travel cost for rotations compared with the budgeted level.

50.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the lower standard troop cost reimbursement and mission subsistence allowance owing to the application of the higher vacancy rate of 13.1 per cent compared with 5.7 per cent applied in the approved budget.

51.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the application of the higher vacancy rate of 8.7 per cent compared with the vacancy rate of 1.4 per cent applied in the approved budget. The reduced requirements are offset in part by the higher mission subsistence allowance rate, effective 1 January 2026, compared with the rates applied in the approved budget.
52.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to higher than budgeted actual separation costs for staff retirement.

53.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the application of the higher vacancy rate of 13.5 per cent compared with the rate of 5.1 per cent applied in the approved budget; (b) the proposed abolishment of two Field Service posts, owing to efficiency measures; and (c) the net impact ($77,400) of the proposed establishment of one P-5 post of Chief of Operations and Resource Management (a vacancy rate of 50 per cent has been applied) and the abolishment of one P-4 post, Chief of Unit Service Delivery, as recommended in the civilian staffing review. The reduced requirements are offset in part by the application of the higher revised salary and common staff cost rates compared with the rates applied in the approved budget.

54.	For the 2024/25 period the increased requirements were attributable primarily to the application of the revised salary scale effective 1 June 2024 promulgated in August 2024, compared with the rates applied in the approved budget.

55.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the higher salary rates based on the revised scale effective 1 November 2025 compared with the rates applied in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period; and (b) the exchange rate of 0.863 euros to 1.0 United States dollar compared with the rate of 0.948 applied in the approved budget. The increased requirements are offset in part by the proposed abolishment of 10 posts, owing to efficiency measures.

56.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to the cost of a temporary replacement position to assist in providing support for human resources services for UNFICYP, the Office of the Special Adviser to the Secretary-General and the Committee on Missing Persons in Cyprus.

57.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the application of the higher salary and common staff cost rates compared with the rates applied in the approved budget.

58.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the non-engagement of individual consultants for intercommunal activities, which was implemented by in-house capacity within the Force.

59.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the postponement of travel requirements, owing to the reprioritization of resources in the light of the liquidity challenges.

60.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to liquidity challenges, which led to the deferral of the acquisition of: (a) safety and security equipment and supplies; (b) certain construction services; and (c) engineering and electrical equipment.

61.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the application of an exchange rate of 0.863 euros to 1.0 United States dollar compared with the rate of 0.948 euros applied in the approved budget; (b) the higher costs of catering services owing to a new contract; (c) a 5 per cent annual increase for cleaning, grounds maintenance and road sweeping services contracts; (d) the higher prices for the maintenance of seven photovoltaic plants stemmed from the revised contract; (e) the higher electricity costs sourced from the host country’s energy grid; and (f) the higher costs of solid waste management services owing to a new contract.

62.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to fewer actual flight hours than the hours budgeted, resulting from the measures implemented in the light of liquidity challenges.

63.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the lower rates for centralized services provided by the Office of Information and Communications Technology, compared with the rates applied in the approved budget, including for video technology conferencing and cloud computing services.

64.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to lower acquisition of medical equipment and consumable medical equipment and supplies resulting from the measures implemented in the light of liquidity challenges.

65.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the application of an exchange rate of 0.863 euros to 1.0 United States dollar compared with the rate of 0.948 euros applied in the approved budget; and (b) the replacement of automated external defibrillators.

66.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) lower-than-budgeted freight costs; (b) fewer interpretation services and reduction in in-person meetings; (c) lower-than-budgeted training activities; and (d) lower requirements for welfare supplies resulting from the measures implemented in the light of liquidity challenges.
67.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to lower requirement for freight owing to fewer planned acquisitions.

text: IV.	Actions to be taken by the General Assembly

children.text: 68.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly in connection with the financing of the United Nations force are:
(a)	To take note of the unencumbered balance of $1,167,100 with respect to the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025;

(b)	To take note of other revenue for the period ended 30 June 2025 amounting to $1,429,000 from investment revenue ($619,600), other/ miscellaneous revenue ($575,400) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($234,000);

(c)	To approve the apportionment of the amount of $2,596,100 among Member States in accordance with the applicable scale of assessment for 2025 and to place such apportioned amount in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates;

(d)	To allow Member States to apply their share of the amount apportioned in subparagraph (c) above towards current or future dues once the General Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has improved sufficiently to allow such application;

(e)	To appropriate the amount of $56,279,700 for the maintenance of the Mission for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027, including $24,383,833 to be funded through voluntary contributions from the Government of Cyprus ($17,883,833) and from the Government of Greece ($6,500,000);
(f)	To assess the amount in subparagraph (e) above at a monthly rate of $2,657,989 should the Security Council decide to continue the mandate of the Mission.

text: V.	Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolutions 76/274 and 79/303, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

children.text: A.	General Assembly
Cross-cutting issues
(Resolution 76/274)

table_content.text: Requests the Secretary-General to improve comprehensive oversight of the activities of peacekeeping missions and implement the recommendations of relevant oversight bodies in this regard to avoid deficiencies in management and related economic losses, with the aim of ensuring full compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations (para. 17).

As at 31 December 2025, 12 of the 20 recommendations (60 per cent) of the Board of Auditors issued in the 2024/25 period and in earlier periods had been implemented. Nine of the 16 recommendations (56 per cent) of the Office of Internal Oversight Services issued in the 2025/26 period and in earlier periods were in the process of implementation. Regular follow-up is undertaken to ensure the timely closure of the remaining recommendations.

Requests that future budget proposals demonstrate the scalability of mission support components, including their staffing and operational costs, be proportionate in relation to the changing level of other mission components and include standard indicators (para. 27).
The mission support ratio (the total number of mission support and security personnel per 1,000 total mission personnel) in the 2025/26 period is 109.9, compared with 110.9 in the 2020/21 period.

Requests the Secretary-General to consider options for greater nationalization of functions when formulating budget submissions, commensurate with mission mandates and requirements where applicable (para. 34).

The Force will continue to review its staffing requirements in line with its priorities and endeavour to nationalize posts whenever feasible.
UNFICYP continues to build on past efforts in this area, and 75.2 per cent of its civilian staffing is composed of national staff.

children.text: B.	Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions
Financing of the United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus
(A/79/724/Add.2 and General Assembly resolution 79/303)

table_content.text: The Advisory Committee notes that, with the exception of UNFICYP, the other peacekeeping missions provide information on official within-mission travel. The Committee is of the view that the presentation of information on official within-mission travel for UNIFICYP would enhance transparency and consistency with the budgeting format across all peacekeeping missions. The Committee trusts that the next UNFICYP budget submission will include information on official within-mission travel (para. 32).
The information on official within-mission travel is presented in attachment D.4 of the supplementary information package.

The Advisory Committee acknowledges the efforts of the Force to improve its environmental footprint and trusts that further efficiency gains will continue to be generated from the photovoltaic power plants, as well as other environmental initiatives, and will be reflected in future reports (para. 33).

For the 2026/27 period, the Force proposed the installation of the ninth photovoltaic power plant in sector 1, combined with the gradual replacement of inefficient electrical appliances, such as air conditioning units with eco-friendly units.

The Advisory Committee recalls General Assembly resolution 76/274 and considers that, as a matter of principle, the Secretary-General should ensure the continuous optimization of the staffing structures, including through civilian and security staffing reviews, with the aim of identifying efficiencies that will be reflected in the next budget proposal. The Committee looks forward to the outcome of the UNFICYP civilian staffing review, in close cooperation with United Nations Headquarters. The Committee trusts that efficiencies, including with respect to the Programme Management Officer (P-4) position for mine action activities, will be identified and reflected in the next budget proposal (para. 34).

Following the civilian staffing review conducted in the 2024/25 period for UNFICYP, the Force has proposed a number of organizational and staffing changes, as reflected in paragraphs 32 to 38 of the present report.
